output:
# Amortized (Direct-Sum) List Sample Compression

List learning generalizes prediction by letting a learner output a short list of $k$ candidate labels and counting it correct if the true label appears in the list — the natural model for recommenders, top-$k$ retrieval, and label-size-independent boosting. In ordinary (binary/multiclass) learning, three pillars coincide: PAC learnability, uniform convergence, and *sample compression* (the Occam-style principle "a hypothesis reconstructible from a few training points generalizes"). List learning fractures this coincidence. Hanneke, Moran, and Waknine (2024) prove that list uniform convergence stays equivalent to list learnability, yet *exact* sample compression is provably **incomplete** — there are 2-list-learnable classes over $\{0,1,2\}$ with **no** compression scheme, even with arbitrarily large reconstruction lists. This work studies that dissociation and proposes to repair the compression pillar by changing its *unit of accounting* from per-instance to per-batch — turning the very direct-sum mechanism that breaks one-shot compression into the resource that restores a complete compression-style generalization principle for every list-learnable class.

## The Five Core Questions

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Is there a relaxation of sample compression — specifically **amortized (direct-sum) compression**, which jointly compresses predictions across a batch of $m$ instances at a sublinear per-instance budget — that is **complete** for list learning, recovering an Occam-style generalization guarantee for *every* list-learnable class and collapsing to standard Littlestone–Warmuth sample compression in the multiclass (k=1, bounded label space) case?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

**For the community: re-opening compression-based design for list learners.** Sample compression is not only a proof technique; it is a *generative* principle. In the binary and multiclass worlds, the compression $\Rightarrow$ generalization bridge underwrites a whole toolkit — Occam/union-bound generalization bounds, margin bounds, boosting-to-compression pipelines, and even differential privacy constructed *via* compression (compressed hypotheses depend on few examples, the key to privacy and stability arguments). The source paper's impossibility result quietly closes that toolkit for list learning: with no complete compression principle, none of these compression-derived algorithms are available off the shelf. Establishing that **amortized** compression is complete for every list-learnable class would *re-open* this entire line of algorithm design for the list setting, now in label-size-independent form. A paper of this shape changes how subsequent work reasons about list and multiclass problems: it converts a dead end ("compression fails for lists") into a usable design surface ("compression succeeds once you account per batch"), and it supplies a new complexity measure — an amortized / direct-sum compression dimension $d_{\mathrm{amort}}$ — that future characterizations can target alongside the list-DS dimension.

**Advancing knowledge: a principle-level reframe.** The result reframes a negative theorem as a statement about the *unit of accounting*. This is a transferable lesson: when a one-shot resource is provably insufficient, the direct-sum structure that defeats it may itself be the resource that restores completeness under amortization. That stance imports the long tradition of direct-sum and information-complexity reasoning (where the cost of solving many copies of a problem jointly is the central object) into statistical learning theory, suggesting a broader program of "amortized" learning principles. It also predicts concrete, checkable consequences — e.g., on the source's $\{0,1,2\}$ 2-list counterexample, exact ($m=1$) compression is impossible, so the theory predicts a strictly positive *minimal batch size* $m^\star > 1$ at which amortized compression first succeeds, turning an abstract completeness claim into a falsifiable quantitative prediction.

**Practical applications: batched prediction is the realistic regime.** List learning's motivating applications — recommender systems, top-$k$ retrieval and ranking, and list/multiclass boosting — *inherently* serve **batches** of queries rather than isolated single instances. A recommender scores many items for many users at once; a retrieval system returns top-$k$ lists for streams of queries. Amortized prediction is therefore not an artifact introduced for mathematical convenience but the **native deployment model**, which makes the theory application-aligned: the per-batch budget the theorem optimizes is exactly the resource a deployed system spends. Coupled with the label-size-independent sample complexity available from list boosting (Brukhim–Hanneke–Moran 2023), a complete amortized compression principle promises generalization guarantees for large- or unbounded-label list learners that match how those systems are actually run — closing the gap between the combinatorial characterization of learnability and the algorithmic, deployable practice of list prediction.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

This is hard for five concrete, interlocking reasons. The difficulty is not incidental — it is structural, and the obvious shortcuts are *provably* dead ends.

**(a) The source already kills the naive object, so any complete principle must be a genuinely new object — not a tweak.**
Hanneke–Moran–Waknine (2024) prove that *exact* sample compression is incomplete for list learning: there is a 2-list-learnable class over labels $\{0,1,2\}$ with **no** compression scheme **even when the reconstruction is allowed arbitrary-size lists**, and a 1-list class with unbounded labels that also fails. This forecloses the two "free knob" relaxations a reader would reach for first:
- *"Just allow bigger reconstruction lists ($K > k$)."* Already permitted in the counterexample — it does not help. So enlarging $K$ is not the missing resource.
- *"Just randomize / use fractional compression."* The impossibility is engineered by **direct-sum** arguments, not by an adversary exploiting determinism. Randomized or stable/replicable compression are generic robustness knobs orthogonal to the direct-sum mechanism; there is no reason they engage the failure, and the source's construction survives them. They are unmotivated for *this* obstruction.

The only relaxation that touches the actual mechanism is to change the **accounting unit** from per-instance to per-batch (amortization). That is precisely why the object must be defined freshly ($\kappa$ produces one certificate scored against a *joint* batch reconstruction), not patched.

**(b) The definition has to thread a needle between two anchors that pull in opposite directions.**
The rate/accounting must satisfy *both*:
- the **collapse anchor** (definitional): at $m=1$ the amortized object *is* ordinary sample compression — for **binary (VC, k=1, bounded $Y$)** it reduces to a standard Littlestone–Warmuth bounded-size scheme; for **multiclass k=1 with $|Y|>2$** the $m=1$ object is ordinary compression, which is *itself* incomplete (Pabbaraju 2023), so the theory predicts $m^\star>1$ even at $k=1$ there; and
- the **m=1 impossibility**: on the $\{0,1,2\}$, $k=2$ class, an $m=1$ scheme cannot exist, so the definition must *predict a finite minimal batch size $m^\star(H) > 1$* at which savings first appear — it must not accidentally "succeed" at $m=1$ (which would contradict the source) nor require $m\to\infty$ trivially (which would make the rate vacuous).

A definition that is too generous (e.g., charging side bits $b$ sublinearly but letting $b$ secretly encode $m$ labels) collapses the rate to a tautology; a definition too strict re-derives the impossibility. The clean structural guard is that **$\kappa$ is query-independent**: it produces the certificate $c=(S',b)$ from the training sample $S$ *before* the query batch $x_1\ldots x_m$ is revealed, so $b$ cannot encode the labels of as-yet-unseen queries; coupled with $\beta(m)=o(m)$ and an **$m$-independent** reconstruction-list bound $K$, this makes $r(m)=(s(m)\cdot\log|\Sigma_{\mathrm{eff}}|+\beta(m))/m$ an *honest* per-instance cost — and so $m^\star$ lands strictly between $2$ and $\infty$ — which is itself a non-trivial modeling theorem, not a convenience.

**(c) The completeness direction needs amortized — not multiplied — certificate information, and that is the crux.**
The proof must show finite list-DS dimension $d \Rightarrow$ a scheme of rate $\tilde{O}(d\cdot k)/m$. The natural pipeline (batched list-boosting à la Brukhim–Hanneke–Moran, then sample-net / one-inclusion aggregation à la Moran–Yehudayoff) produces a certificate per round. The danger: the side information $b$ that records per-round example indices and vote thresholds is naturally **multiplied** across the $m$ queries (size $\sim m \times$ single-shot), giving rate $\Theta(1)$, no savings. Completeness *requires* proving that the shared combinatorial structure (one shattered/list-DS configuration) is encoded **once** and reused across the whole batch, so certificate size $\asymp$ (single-shot class information) $+\, o(m)$. Separating **shared** information (charged once) from **per-instance** information (charged $m$ times) is exactly the direct-sum bookkeeping the source used destructively — we must run it constructively, and there is no off-the-shelf lemma that does this for list-DS configurations. This is the single hardest step.

**(d) Matching lower bounds and the agnostic extension are not free.**
A vanishing-rate *upper* bound is only half an equivalence. We also need a $\Omega(d\cdot k/m)$ **lower bound**, obtained by *reversing* the source's direct-sum impossibility into a rate floor — but adversary constructions that lower-bound joint/amortized cost are delicate (an over-strong adversary re-proves impossibility; an over-weak one gives a loose floor). The **agnostic** extension compounds this: realizable compression bounds do not transfer to agnostic for free, and list agnostic learning lacks a clean ERM, so the expected extra $O(\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)/m})$ term must be earned through a separate uniform-convergence-style argument that still respects the amortized accounting.

**(e) Unbounded label space may be a genuine barrier, not a gap in our proof.**
The source's 1-list unbounded-label failure suggests amortization might *not* rescue compression when $|Y|$ is unbounded: the effective alphabet $\Sigma_{\mathrm{eff}}$ (the realized label set on $S$) grows, inflating $s(m)\cdot\log|\Sigma_{\mathrm{eff}}|$. So the honest expectation is that completeness holds for **bounded** $Y$ and that unbounded $Y$ is either a real separation or requires a different, alphabet-free accounting. Pretending otherwise risks claiming a false theorem; treating it as a possible dichotomy is the rigorous stance.

**Net:** straightforward approaches fail because the obstruction is direct-sum/amortization-structural, the two sanity anchors actively constrain the definition, and the key inequality (amortized $\ll$ multiplied certificate size) is the very phenomenon the source exploited to prove impossibility. We have to invert that mechanism, with controlled bookkeeping, and prove a matching floor.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

**The gap.** The entire lineage of learning theory — VC/PAC, DS-dimension, list-DS dimension, list online learning, list boosting — answers the question *which classes are learnable*. Hanneke–Moran–Waknine (2024) sharpen this to a separation: uniform convergence is complete for list learning, but exact sample compression is not. What no prior work asks is the natural *successor* question that the separation forces: once exact compression collapses, **which compression-style algorithmic principle, if any, becomes complete again?** Characterizing learnability tells you a class *can* be learned; it does not restore the Occam/compression bridge — "short certificate $\Rightarrow$ generalization" — that makes compression valuable as a *design* principle. That bridge is exactly what the source result severs, and the literature simply stops at the negative statement, explicitly flagging open questions "around list sample compression and direct sums."

**Why progress stalled.** The barrier is conceptual, not merely technical. The impossibility is not caused by a generic deficiency one could patch with a standard relaxation. Two such relaxations are tempting and have rich prior literature — (i) *randomized / fractional* compression schemes, and (ii) *stable / replicable* compression (in the spirit of stability-based generalization à la Bousquet–Hanneke–Moran–Zhivotovskiy 2020) — but each is a generic knob orthogonal to *why* list compression fails. The source's lower bound has nothing to do with randomness or instability; it is a **direct-sum** phenomenon: the joint cost of labeling many points is genuinely smaller than the sum of per-point costs, and exact compression is blind to that gap. A faithful fix must engage the direct-sum mechanism head-on rather than perturb an unrelated axis, and no one had identified that the *accounting unit* — per-instance versus per-batch — was the lever.

**How amortized compression differs and improves.** The novelty is a change of accounting unit. Instead of demanding a single-point certificate (the object the source proves impossible), we ask for an **amortized list compression scheme**: compress the sample once and be scored on labeling a *batch* of $m$ future instances *jointly*, at a budget that is **sublinear per instance** ($o(m)$ retained examples plus $o(m)$ side bits to predict $m$ points). The decisive structural insight is **mechanism reuse**: the very direct-sum / amortization device that Hanneke–Moran–Waknine deploy as the *obstruction* is re-deployed as the *remedy*. Concretely, the shared combinatorial structure of a finite-list-DS class (a shattered configuration) can be encoded *once* and reused across all $m$ queries, so amortized certificate size scales like (single-shot information content) $+\, o(m)$ rather than $m \times$ (single-shot size). This is precisely the regime where the source's per-instance lower bound has no purchase. Thus the contribution is not "compression with a tweak" but the identification of the *right complexity unit* (per-batch) at which a compression principle survives the collapse.

**Contrast with the two weaker candidates, and the honest boundary.** Randomized/fractional compression buys a constant-factor or distributional relaxation but does not touch the direct-sum gap, so it cannot be complete where the gap is the obstruction. Stable/replicable compression strengthens reproducibility but is again orthogonal to the joint-cost mechanism. Amortization is the *unique* candidate that is (a) motivated by the exact proof technique that defeats exact compression, (b) theorem-shaped with a crisp completeness target (finite list-DS $\Rightarrow$ vanishing-rate amortized compression), and (c) anchored by a built-in **definitional** collapse: at $m=1$ the amortized object *is* ordinary compression. Existence of a bounded $m=1$ scheme is recovered only for **VC binary** classes (Moran–Yehudayoff); for genuine **multiclass k=1 with $|Y|>2$** ordinary compression is itself incomplete (Pabbaraju 2023), so the theory predicts $m^\star>1$ even at $k=1$ there — fully consistent with the $\{0,1,2\}$, $k=2$ impossibility (a different $k$ regime, so no contradiction). Importantly, this is a *list, amortized* completeness statement — it does **not** claim to resolve the classical Littlestone–Warmuth sample-compression conjecture, which remains a separate, exact, per-instance question.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

### METHOD (the proof program)

**(1) Definitions and complexity measures.**
- *Amortized list compression scheme of rate $r(m)$:* a pair $(\kappa,\rho)$ where the **query-independent** compression map $\kappa(S)=c=(S',b)$, with $S'\subseteq S$, $|S'|\le s(m)$, and side information $b\in\{0,1\}^{\beta(m)}$, is computed *before* the batch is revealed; the reconstruction map $\rho(c, x_1\ldots x_m)$ outputs joint label-lists $\hat{y}_1\ldots\hat{y}_m$ with $|\hat{y}_i|\le K$ for an **$m$-independent** $K\ge k$; realizable agreement holds on $S$; and $r(m)=(s(m)\cdot\log|\Sigma_{\mathrm{eff}}|+\beta(m))/m$, target $r(m)=o(1)$. Here $\Sigma_{\mathrm{eff}}$ is the realized label set on $S$. Because $\kappa$ does not see the query batch and $\beta(m)=o(m)$, $b$ cannot encode the $m$ query labels — this is what makes the rate honest rather than vacuous.
- *Amortized (direct-sum) compression dimension:* $d_{\mathrm{amort}}(H)=\limsup_m [\text{min certificate size for length-}m\text{ batches}]/m$. The dimension and the rate are the same object viewed two ways: **$d_{\mathrm{amort}}(H)=0 \iff \exists$ a scheme with $r(m)=o(1)$.** **Conjecture:** $d_{\mathrm{amort}}(H)=0 \iff H$ list-learnable $\iff$ finite list-DS dim.
- *Minimal effective batch:* $m^\star(H)=\min m$ at which positive rate-savings exist (predicted finite, $>1$, on the $\{0,1,2\}$ class).

**(2) Completeness construction (finite list-DS $\Rightarrow$ vanishing-rate scheme).**
- *Batched list-boosting:* run a list/multiclass weak-to-strong aggregation (Brukhim–Hanneke–Moran route) over the realizable sample to get $T=\tilde{O}(d\cdot k)$ weak list-hypotheses.
- *Sample-net / one-inclusion aggregation:* via the Moran–Yehudayoff "boosting $\Rightarrow$ bounded compression" route, the strong predictor is represented by a bounded subsample $S'$ plus per-round side info.
- *Amortization step (the crux):* the side bits $b$ encode the per-round example **indices and vote thresholds ONCE**, shared across all $m$ queries; only an $O(\log K)$-per-instance disambiguation is charged per query. This yields $s(m)=\tilde{O}(d\cdot k)$ ($m$-independent core) $+ o(m)$ per-instance overhead, so $r(m)=\tilde{O}(d\cdot k)/m \to 0$.
- *Coding-theoretic bridge:* treat the batch certificate as a channel code for the $m$ true label-lists; list-DS dimension upper-bounds the effective message entropy, giving the rate calculus and unifying with the source's positive (coding-theoretic) UC proof.

**(3) Converse (vanishing-rate scheme $\Rightarrow$ generalization).**
Amortized Occam / union-bound counting over the number of *distinct certificates* of size $s(m)\cdot\log|\Sigma_{\mathrm{eff}}|+\beta(m)$: with sublinear certificate length the certificate count is sub-exponential in $m$, so a Littlestone–Warmuth-style argument lifted to batch certificates gives generalization. One technical caveat must be stated rather than glossed: the $m$ predictions share a *single* certificate, so per-instance errors are **not independent** within a batch. The certificate-count union bound therefore controls **batch-aggregate / expected per-instance** error over a **fresh** batch drawn *after* the certificate is fixed; intra-batch dependence is handled precisely because the certificate is frozen before the fresh batch is drawn. With that acknowledgment this remains the easier of the two directions, but it is not free.

**(4) Lower bound.**
Reverse the source's direct-sum impossibility into a rate **floor** $\Omega(d\cdot k/m)$: an adversary class forces any joint certificate to carry $\Omega(d\cdot k)$ shared bits, no fewer, yielding matching scaling and closing the equivalence (realizable).

**(5) Agnostic extension.**
Realizable scheme + a list uniform-convergence argument $\Rightarrow$ agnostic guarantee with additive $O(\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)/m})$; verify graceful degradation to the realizable bound at the noise-free limit.

### "DATASETS" / TESTBEDS (canonical concept-class families to instantiate the theory)
- **Source's $\{0,1,2\}$ 2-list counterexample** — must exhibit finite $m^\star>1$ (no $m=1$ scheme, savings appear at some $m\ge 2$).
- **Finite list-DS classes** — list versions of halfspaces / thresholds, bounded-degree pattern classes — instantiate the construction and confirm $s(m)=\tilde{O}(d\cdot k)$ core.
- **Unbounded-label constructions** — candidate separations probing barrier (e); test whether rate stays $\Theta(1)$.
- **Littlestone-dimension / VC classes (k=1, bounded $Y$)** — the collapse-check family.

### METRICS
- Per-instance rate $r(m)$ and whether $r(m)\to 0$.
- $d_{\mathrm{amort}}(H)$ (target $0 \iff$ learnable).
- $m^\star(H)$ (finite, $>1$ on $\{0,1,2\}$).
- Certificate-size scaling vs (list-DS dim $d$, list size $k$): target $\tilde{O}(d\cdot k)$.
- Realizable$\to$agnostic gap (the $O(\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)/m})$ term).
- Collapse-identity check: $m=1$, $k=1$, bounded $Y$ reproduces the Littlestone–Warmuth bound within constants (VC binary; for multiclass $|Y|>2$ this is definitional only and predicts $m^\star>1$).

### BASELINES / COMPARISON POINTS
- **Exact ($m=1$) compression** — provably impossible; the target our scheme must beat.
- **Randomized / fractional compression** — argue it does not engage the direct-sum mechanism (counterexample survives it).
- **Stable / replicable compression** — orthogonal robustness knob; does not touch amortization.
- **List uniform convergence** — the principle whose completeness we are matching (the existing $\iff$ we sit beside).

### EXPECTED OUTCOMES (clear, falsifiable)
- **Primary (success):** constructive vanishing-rate scheme for ALL finite-list-DS classes + matching $\Omega(d\cdot k/m)$ lower bound (realizable) $\Rightarrow$ establishes the third $\iff$ in the equivalence chain; plus agnostic extension.
- **Honest fallback:** prove completeness for **bounded** label space + a dichotomy/separation for unbounded labels — a publishable structural result; state plainly which parts are theorem vs conjecture.
- **Refutation check (falsifiability):** a finite-list-DS class with NO vanishing-rate scheme even as $m\to\infty$ would *refute* the conjecture — and is itself a strong negative result. The target is genuinely falsifiable.

### SANITY GATES (mandatory, must all pass)
1. $m=1$, $k=1$, bounded $Y$, **VC binary** $\Rightarrow$ reproduces Littlestone–Warmuth bounded compression (within constants). For **multiclass $k=1$ with $|Y|>2$** the collapse is definitional only — the $m=1$ object is ordinary compression, which is itself incomplete (Pabbaraju 2023), so the gate is "predicts $m^\star>1$," not "produces an $m=1$ scheme."
2. Scheme must NOT contradict the source's $m=1$ $\{0,1,2\}$, $k=2$ impossibility $\Rightarrow$ predicts finite $m^\star>1$.
3. Agnostic bound degrades gracefully to realizable as noise $\to 0$.

### HONEST DIFFICULTY ASSESSMENT
The completeness direction (step 2, amortization) is genuinely hard and is where the proposal could fail. The converse (with the intra-batch-correlation caveat above), the collapse anchor, and the bounded-label fallback are on firmer ground. Unbounded labels are an open barrier we treat as a possible separation, not an assumed theorem. No claim resolves the classical Littlestone–Warmuth conjecture; this is a strictly list, amortized statement.

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

The statistical theory of learning begins with the PAC framework and the Vapnik–Chervonenkis (VC) dimension, which Blumer, Ehrenfeucht, Haussler, and Warmuth distilled into the fundamental theorem of binary classification: finite VC dimension is equivalent to uniform convergence and to PAC learnability. A parallel, algorithmic account of *why* learning generalizes was opened by Littlestone and Warmuth (1986), who introduced **sample compression** — if a learner can reconstruct its hypothesis from a bounded subset of the training data, then it generalizes — and conjectured that finite VC dimension always yields a compression scheme of size bounded in the VC dimension. Moran and Yehudayoff (2016) proved a near-resolution: every VC class admits a sample compression scheme of size exponential in the VC dimension ($2^{O(d)}$), establishing compression as an essentially *complete* generalization principle for binary classification, though the exact **linear $O(d)$** size — the open Warmuth conjecture — remains unresolved. Moving beyond binary labels, Daniely and Shalev-Shwartz introduced the multiclass analogue and the seeds of the **Daniely–Shalev-Shwartz (DS) dimension**, and Brukhim, Carmon, Dinur, Moran, and Yehudayoff (2022) proved that finite DS dimension exactly characterizes multiclass PAC learnability, settling a long-standing question and revealing that the DS dimension — not any VC-style combinatorial shattering — is the correct measure.

The **list-learning** model then generalizes prediction itself: the learner may output a short *list* of $k$ candidate labels and is correct if the true label appears in the list. Charikar and Pabbaraju (2022) introduced the **list-DS dimension** and characterized list PAC learnability by its finiteness; Moran, Sharon, Tsubari, and Yosebashvili (2023) developed the online/Littlestone counterpart for list prediction; and Brukhim, Hanneke, and Moran (2023) built multiclass and list boosting machinery with sample complexity that can be made *independent of the label-set size*. A separate but crucial thread is Pabbaraju (2023), who showed that *multiclass* learnability does **not** imply sample compression — exact compression is already incomplete at list size $k=1$ once $|Y|>2$ — extending the compression lower bounds that the source paper builds on.

Against this backdrop, Hanneke, Moran, and Waknine (2024) prove a sharp **dissociation**: list uniform convergence remains equivalent to list learnability (a positive, coding-theoretic result), yet *exact* sample compression is provably **incomplete** for list learning — there exist 2-list-learnable classes over the three-symbol alphabet $\{0,1,2\}$ for which *no* compression scheme exists, even when the reconstruction is allowed an arbitrarily large output list, with an analogous failure for 1-list learning over unbounded label spaces. This refutes the natural list-compression conjecture, extends the compression lower bounds of Pabbaraju (2023), and — most importantly for the present work — engineers its impossibility through **direct-sum** arguments, showing that jointly handling many instances is the mechanism that defeats one-shot compression.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

**The central dissociation.** The organizing fact is a clean split inside list learning: *uniform convergence holds* (it stays equivalent to list learnability, by the source's positive coding-theoretic argument) while *exact sample compression fails* (provably incomplete, by the source's $\{0,1,2\}$ direct-sum counterexample). Two pillars that are inseparable in binary/multiclass learning come apart in the list regime. This is the gap the proposal targets: not learnability (already characterized by the finite list-DS dimension) but the *algorithmic principle* — the Occam/compression bridge — that the negative result severs.

**The mechanism-reuse thesis.** The impossibility is engineered by direct sums: the joint cost of labeling many points is strictly smaller than $m\times$ the per-point cost, and a *per-instance* certificate is blind to that gap. The proposal's thesis is that this very gap is the resource. Encode the shared combinatorial structure of a finite-list-DS class **once** and reuse it across the whole batch, so the certificate size is (single-shot information) $+\, o(m)$ rather than $m\times$ (single-shot size). The same direct-sum device is thus both the obstruction (per instance) and the remedy (per batch) — a structural reframe, not a parameter tweak.

**The target equivalence chain.** The proposal seeks to extend the known equivalence with a third conjunct (the new one in **bold**):

> $H$ is list-PAC-learnable $\iff$ $H$ has finite list-DS dimension $\iff$ **$H$ admits an amortized list compression scheme of vanishing rate $r(m)=o(1)$** $\iff$ $H$ satisfies list uniform convergence.

The new conjunct is anchored by two equivalent views of one object: the **rate** $r(m)\to 0$ and the **amortized dimension** $d_{\mathrm{amort}}(H)=0$ ($d_{\mathrm{amort}}(H)=0 \iff \exists$ scheme with $r(m)=o(1)$). The completeness (hard) direction is finite list-DS $\Rightarrow$ rate $\tilde{O}(d\cdot k)/m$; the converse (compression $\Rightarrow$ generalization) is an amortized Occam count over distinct certificates, subject to the intra-batch-correlation caveat (the bound controls expected per-instance error on a fresh batch drawn after the certificate is fixed). A finer parameter $m^\star(H)$ — the minimal batch size at which positive rate savings first appear — is predicted finite and $>1$ on the source's counterexample.

**Honest difficulty / feasibility.** Feasibility is uneven and the proposal labels it so. The collapse anchor is **definitional** and self-consistent: at $m=1$ the amortized object *is* ordinary compression, and soundness (compression $\Rightarrow$ generalization) always holds; *existence* of a bounded $m=1$ scheme is recovered only for VC binary classes, while genuine multiclass $k=1$ ($|Y|>2$) is itself incomplete (Pabbaraju 2023), so the theory predicts $m^\star>1$ even there — consistent with, not contradicting, the $k=2$ $\{0,1,2\}$ impossibility. The converse and the bounded-label fallback are on firm ground. The completeness construction (amortized, not multiplied, certificate information) is the genuine crux and the most likely failure point: there is no off-the-shelf lemma separating shared from per-instance information for list-DS configurations. Unbounded label space is treated as a possible *barrier*, not an assumed theorem. Crucially, no claim resolves the classical Littlestone–Warmuth conjecture; the statement is strictly *list* and *amortized*.

## 3. Novel Insights and Recommendations

**The change-of-accounting-unit insight.** The core idea is to stop asking "how few bits per instance?" and ask "how few bits per *batch*?" The negative result is a per-instance statement; compression is incomplete **per instance** but conjecturally complete **per batch**. Reframing the impossibility as a statement about the *unit of accounting* — and recognizing that the direct-sum structure defeating one-shot compression is itself the resource that restores completeness under amortization — is the transferable, principle-level contribution. It imports direct-sum / information-complexity reasoning into statistical learning theory.

**Falsifiable predictions.** The proposal is genuinely testable. (i) On the source's $\{0,1,2\}$, $k=2$ counterexample, exact ($m=1$) compression is impossible, so the theory predicts a finite minimal batch size $m^\star>1$ at which amortized compression first succeeds. (ii) For **multiclass $k=1$ with $|Y|>2$**, since ordinary compression is incomplete (Pabbaraju 2023), the theory predicts $m^\star>1$ *even at $k=1$* — the collapse there is definitional, not an existence claim. (iii) Certificate-size should scale as $\tilde{O}(d\cdot k)$ in the shared core with $o(m)$ per-instance overhead, giving $r(m)\to 0$.

**Primary result vs honest fallback vs refutation check.**
- *Primary (success):* a constructive vanishing-rate scheme for all finite-list-DS classes plus a matching $\Omega(d\cdot k/m)$ lower bound (realizable), establishing the third $\iff$, with an agnostic extension carrying an additive $O(\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)/m})$ term.
- *Honest fallback:* if full completeness resists, prove it for **bounded** label space and exhibit a dichotomy/separation for **unbounded** labels — already a publishable structural result; state plainly which parts are theorem vs conjecture.
- *Refutation check:* a finite-list-DS class admitting **no** vanishing-rate scheme even as $m\to\infty$ would *refute* the conjecture and itself be a strong negative result — so the target is genuinely falsifiable.

**Recommended proof-program order.** (1) Lock the definition so the two anchors hold and the rate is honest (query-independent $\kappa$; $\beta(m)=o(m)$; $m$-independent $K$; $\Sigma_{\mathrm{eff}}$ as the realized label set). (2) Prove the converse and the collapse identity first (firmest ground, validates the definition). (3) Attack the completeness construction via batched list-boosting $\to$ one-inclusion aggregation $\to$ the amortization (shared-once) step — the crux. (4) Establish the matching lower bound by reversing the source's direct-sum construction. (5) Extend to agnostic. Run the sanity gates continuously.

**Toward an "amortized learning principles" program.** Beyond this single theorem, the stance suggests a research program: whenever a one-shot algorithmic resource is provably insufficient for a learning task, ask whether its direct-sum/amortized analogue is complete. Amortized compression for list learning would be the first concrete instance, supplying a new complexity measure ($d_{\mathrm{amort}}$) that future characterizations can target alongside the list-DS dimension, and re-opening compression-based design (Occam bounds, boosting-to-compression, privacy-via-compression) for the list and large-label regimes.

## 4. References and Evidence

- Vapnik, V. N., & Chervonenkis, A. Ya. (1971). *On the Uniform Convergence of Relative Frequencies of Events to Their Probabilities.* (Foundational VC theory.)
- Blumer, A., Ehrenfeucht, A., Haussler, D., & Warmuth, M. K. (1989). *Learnability and the Vapnik–Chervonenkis Dimension.* Journal of the ACM. (Year/venue best-known; the fundamental theorem of PAC learning.)
- Littlestone, N., & Warmuth, M. K. (1986). *Relating Data Compression and Learnability.* (Unpublished manuscript; origin of sample compression and the sample-compression conjecture.)
- Moran, S., & Yehudayoff, A. (2016). *Sample Compression Schemes for VC Classes.* Journal of the ACM / earlier conference version. (Bounded-size — $2^{O(d)}$ — compression for VC classes.)
- Daniely, A., & Shalev-Shwartz, S. (2014). *Optimal Learners for Multiclass Problems.* (Introduces the multiclass / DS-style dimension; year best-known, marked uncertain.)
- Brukhim, N., Carmon, D., Dinur, I., Moran, S., & Yehudayoff, A. (2022). *A Characterization of Multiclass Learnability.* (DS dimension characterizes multiclass PAC learnability.)
- Charikar, M., & Pabbaraju, C. (2022). *A Characterization of List Learnability.* (Introduces the list-DS dimension; load-bearing for the first two $\iff$ in the equivalence chain — attribution best-known, marked uncertain, a verified citation would strengthen the backbone.)
- Moran, S., Sharon, O., Tsubari, I., & Yosebashvili, S. (2023). *List Online Classification.* (List Littlestone / online list learning; author list and title best-known, marked uncertain.)
- Brukhim, N., Hanneke, S., & Moran, S. (2023). *Multiclass Boosting and the Cost of Weak Learning* / list boosting with label-size-independent sample complexity. (Title best-known, marked uncertain.)
- Pabbaraju, C. (2023). *Multiclass Learnability Does Not Imply Sample Compression.* (Compression lower bounds extended by the source paper; establishes that exact compression is incomplete already at $k=1$, $|Y|>2$; title best-known, marked uncertain.)
- Bousquet, O., Hanneke, S., Moran, S., & Zhivotovskiy, N. (2020). *Proper Learning, Helly Number, and an Optimal SVM Bound* / stability and compression line. (Cited for the stability-based candidate relaxation; exact title/year best-known, marked uncertain.)
- Hanneke, S., Moran, S., & Waknine, T. (2024). *List Sample Compression and Uniform Convergence.* arXiv:2403.10889. (The source paper.)

*Note on uncertainty:* Where exact years, venues, or full author lists are not verified, entries are marked "best-known / marked uncertain" and identified by author + title only. No arXiv IDs or DOIs are fabricated; the sole verified identifier given is arXiv:2403.10889 for the source paper.
